policy_statement: Install Visual Guidance Docking System (VGDS) at airports to reduce aircraft idling.
mechanism_description: Deploying visual docking guidance at airport gates reduces aircraft ground-idling time, cutting fuel use and emissions from airport operations.
typology_evidence_quote: "Install Visual Guidance Docking System (VGDS) at airports to reduce aircraft idling."
primary_causal_pathway: Operational change (faster gate docking/turnaround) → ↓ aircraft idling/APU/taxi fuel burn → ↓ CO₂e emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in activity/system: Airport gate operations are optimized (e.g., installing visual docking guidance systems and related procedures) so arriving aircraft can align with gates faster and more reliably, reducing time spent stationary with engines running and/or using the auxiliary power unit (APU).  
2) Mediating physical process: Less time running engines/APU on the ground leads to less combustion of aviation fuel (and reduced on-ground power demand where applicable).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower fuel combustion results in reduced CO₂ (and related) emissions from aircraft ground operations.
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing aircraft ground-idling time lowers jet fuel consumption, producing a direct reduction in GHG emissions from airport operations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Gate docking optimization reduces ground-idling time, which reduces fuel burn and thus directly reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can be framed as operational efficiency, the single biggest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is the direct decrease in combustion emissions; that is Mitigation rather than Adaptation or Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s main intent were congestion/air quality with negligible fuel impacts, categorization could be less clear, but the mechanism as defined explicitly targets idling/fuel/emissions reductions.  
4) Applies to all instances: Any implementation of gate docking optimization that reduces idling time will, by the same physical relationship between idling duration and fuel consumption, reduce GHG emissions—so Mitigation is consistent across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: More granular subtyping (e.g., aviation operations, ground support equipment interactions, air quality co-benefits) depends on policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If aircraft substitute reduced engine-idle time with increased APU use (or if gate electrification/ground power availability is the true driver), the mechanism could partially shift toward broader operational energy efficiency; however, the dominant climate effect remains reduced fuel-related emissions from ground operations.
key_indicators: "Install Visual Guidance Docking System (VGDS)"; "at airports"; "to reduce aircraft idling"
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants (NOx/PM) and noise from less idling/APU use; improved gate efficiency/turnaround reliability
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as procurement of airport equipment, but the core action is capital installation/upgrade, so infrastructure-investment is the closest fit.
